title: The Book of Faith
summary: The checked-in ajh1649 XML is a local representation of University of Michigan CME item AJH1649.0001.001, an uncorrected 2006 OCR linked to 316 page images of J. L. Morison's 1909 edition. Morison's first objective was a complete text, transcribed directly from the sole known witness, Trinity College Cambridge MS B.14.45; he retained manuscript spelling subject to stated exceptions, supplied all punctuation, and used Henry Wharton's 1688 partial transcript only for isolated comparisons rather than as his continuous base. Wharton independently transcribed selected Part I excerpts and the surviving Part II from B.14.45, and that print was later encoded as EEBO-TCP A53931. Complete public local caches now preserve the Morison scan, the EEBO-TCP XML, and all 260 canvases of Trinity's B.14.45 IIIF object. Current Trinity foliation 1r–126v and Morison/U-M legacy milestones 1a–129b remain parallel because an exact surface crosswalk is unresolved.

entities.label: General Corpus source XML for ajh1649
entities.description: The exact XML artifact consumed by this repository's conversion and publication workflow, supplied through the Kyvero-Press/CME git submodule.
notes: The mixed-case U-M identifier and lowercase repository filename designate the same item representation.
The file includes placeholders for omitted pages, transcribed title matter, uncorrected OCR of the work, inline edition notes, page links, and Morison's legacy folio milestones.

The pinned CME root LICENSE is the Unlicense and repeatedly describes its subject as software. No path-specific statement was found expressly applying that grant to the inherited U-M XML, data, transcription, or markup, so its scope for this artifact remains ambiguous.

entities.label: University of Michigan CME item AJH1649.0001.001
entities.description: A public TEI-in-Libraries Level 1 electronic text created by uncorrected OCR from linked page images of Morison's 1909 edition. It transcribes title matter and the complete surviving Book of Faith text while representing most other edition pages only as omitted-page milestones.

date_statements.label: U-M publication and header creation
date_statements.notes: The header records creation by mrcb2teiutf.xsl on 2006-02-22.

notes: This item predates the collection's 2016–2018 manually keyed batch; its item-specific editorial declaration expressly says OCR, no correction, and no content editing.

entities.label: U-M page-image set for AJH1649.0001.001
entities.description: The historical production set of 316 linked 600 dpi TIFF G4 page images from which U-M created its 2006 OCR, covering the complete scanned Morison volume. The XML preserves page identifiers and historical image metadata, but a current exact image delivery route was not verified.
entities.reproduction_format: Historically described as 316 600 dpi TIFF G4 page images
notes: The target text is represented on image sequences 108–305; sequences 1–107 and 306–316 are covers, title/preliminary matter, or back matter outside the cataloged work boundary.
The XML establishes that this complete image layer existed when the OCR package was produced; it does not by itself prove that the TIFFs remain currently retrievable.

entities.label: Morison's 1909 edition of The Book of Faith

bibliographic.citation: Reginald Pecock, Reginald Pecock's Book of Faith: A Fifteenth Century Theological Tractate, edited from the MS. in the Library of Trinity College, Cambridge, with an introductory essay by J. L. Morison (Glasgow: James MacLehose and Sons, 1909).
bibliographic.title: Reginald Pecock's Book of Faith: A Fifteenth Century Theological Tractate

notes: The complete surviving medieval text occupies printed pp. 109–305. Morison's preface, introductory essay, summary, table of authorities, glossary, and indexes are distinct editorial matter.

Morison says the first object was a complete text; manuscript spelling was retained except for stated obvious absurdity and a symbol substitution, while punctuation is entirely editorial and a few bare textual explanations are supplied.
Morison reports the manuscript as 127 folios and repeats an older early-fifteenth-century dating. The current Trinity catalog instead records 126 folios and only the fifteenth century.

entities.label: Internet Archive scan of Morison's 1909 edition
entities.description: A complete 324-page PDF derivative of the Princeton Theological Seminary Library copy, including covers, preliminary matter, the full surviving text, notes, glossary, indexes, and terminal matter.
entities.reproduction_format: Scanned page images rendered as PDF with OCR metadata
notes: The target work maps to printed pp. 109–305 and PDF pages 113–309.

entities.label: Trinity College Cambridge MS B.14.45
entities.description: The sole known manuscript witness of The Book of Faith, a fifteenth-century parchment codex written 22 lines to a page. It lacks two leaves in quire 2 and all remaining material after current f.126v, so the text is internally lacunose and ends imperfectly.

date_statements.label: current institutional dating
date_statements.notes: The normalized lower bound reflects the work's c.1456–57 composition within the catalog's broad fifteenth-century date; no narrower paleographical range is asserted.

holding.notes: Given by Archbishop John Whitgift. A flyleaf title calls the contents Reginald Pecock's sermons in English and marks them imperfect; a modern pencil note names Stowe.

physical_description.extent: 126 folios in the current Trinity catalog
notes: Twenty-two lines to a page; the catalog calls it well written.
Collation: 1^8 2^8 (wants 4, 5) 3^8–16^8, rest gone.
The prologue ends on f.8; Part I has ten chapters and ends on f.79; Part II begins on f.79v, has five surviving chapters, and ends imperfectly on f.126v.
Marginal notes occur in a small fifteenth-century hand, with additions in lower margins by the original hand.
Current fols.1r–126v and Morison's legacy 1a–129b milestones describe the same witness. They remain separate locator systems pending a complete crosswalk.
The current catalog explicitly calls B.14.45 the only known manuscript of the treatise.

entities.label: Trinity College complete IIIF facsimile of B.14.45
entities.description: The complete 260-canvas color digital object from front cover through back cover, including flyleaves and every surviving surface from f.1r to f.126v.
entities.reproduction_format: IIIF Presentation 2 manifest with JPEG derivatives from 2420 × 3277 source images
notes: Complete refers to all provider canvases for the present codex, not restoration of the leaves and ending already missing from the physical witness.

entities.label: Trinity College Cambridge catalog description of B.14.45
entities.description: The current institutional manuscript description used for shelfmark, sole-witness status, fifteenth-century date, physical structure, contents, losses, provenance, bibliography, and IIIF discovery.

entities.label: Wharton's 1688 partial edition of The Book of Faith

bibliographic.citation: Reginald Pecock, A Treatise Proving Scripture to Be the Rule of Faith, writ by Reginald Peacock ... before the Reformation, about the year MCDL, edited with a preface and glossary by Henry Wharton (London: printed for James Adamson, 1688).
bibliographic.title: A Treatise Proving Scripture to Be the Rule of Faith

notes: Wharton says he transcribed B.14.45 directly. He omitted the prologue and most of Part I as too obscure, printed selected Part I fragments, and printed Part II complete as far as the imperfect manuscript allowed.
The publication is an actual partial historical transmission of Pecock's work, not merely later commentary.

entities.label: EEBO microfilm page images of the 1688 Wharton edition
entities.description: Forty-four 1-bit TIFF page images scanned from Early English Books microfilm of the Harvard University Libraries source copy and exposed through Historical Texts/EEBO. The image service is separate from the CC0 TCP text.
entities.reproduction_format: 44 one-bit group-IV TIFF page images from microfilm

entities.label: EEBO-TCP Phase I text A53931
entities.description: A manually keyboarded and encoded text produced from 44 EEBO page images of Wharton's 1688 volume and released publicly through the Oxford Text Archive under CC0. It encodes Wharton's preface, partial Pecock transcription, glossary, and associated print matter.
notes: Complete means the full available A53931 XML, not the complete medieval Book of Faith; the 1688 source itself contains only Wharton's selected transmission.

entities.label: Kelly's 2021 study of the judicial processes against Pecock
entities.description: A peer-reviewed modern article used for current chronology and context. It dates The Book of Faith c.1456–57 and identifies both Morison's 1909 edition and Smith's 2020 modern English translation, but it is not a textual source for the repository artifact.

bibliographic.citation: Henry Ansgar Kelly, ‘Judicial Processes for and against Bishop Reginald Pecock: New Perspectives on the Mechanisms of His Downfall,’ Viator 52.1 (2021): 359–430, doi:10.1484/J.VIATOR.5.130891.
bibliographic.title: Judicial Processes for and against Bishop Reginald Pecock: New Perspectives on the Mechanisms of His Downfall

entities.label: eScholarship PDF of Kelly's 2021 article
entities.description: The complete 73-page repository PDF delivery copy of Kelly's article. It is cached as a supporting scholarly source and does not reproduce the target medieval work or manuscript.

entities.label: U.S. Copyright Office Circular 15A (April 2026)
entities.description: Official U.S. Copyright Office guidance used only for the United States public-domain term assessment of the 1688 Wharton and 1909 Morison publications.
notes: catalog_description is a provisional schema workaround for a separately delivered legal-guidance source; the circular contains no target-work text.

scope.notes: The title page says edited from the Trinity manuscript. Morison's preface states that a complete text was his first objective, specifies retention of manuscript spelling subject to stated exceptions, and identifies punctuation and limited explanation as his own interventions.

object.description: Corresponding readings in Wharton's partial transcript

scope.notes: This narrow relation does not make Wharton Morison's continuous base. Morison's continuous source is B.14.45, and his preface calls Wharton's transcript partial and careless.

scope.notes: Wharton expressly says the treatise was transcribed by him from the manuscript at Trinity College Cambridge and explains his omissions and selected coverage.

rights.component: Pinned CME repository XML serialization, including contributor-owned repository additions and inherited U-M transcription and markup
rights.jurisdiction: Repository license assertion; inherited-source scope unresolved
rights.basis: The pinned CME root LICENSE is the Unlicense and broadly permits use and dedicates copyright interests in 'software.' It does not expressly identify the imported U-M XML corpus, data, transcription, encoding, or markup as covered software, and no path-specific notice was found. The exact license assertion is retained for contributor-owned software and repository contributions without treating it as blanket permission for inherited material or as overriding upstream terms.
notes: The Unlicense assertion is attributed to CME for contributor-owned software and repository contributions; this record does not infer that CME contributors own or can relicense inherited U-M XML, data, transcription, encoding, or markup.

U-M's current No Copyright–United States assertion remains separately attached to the U-M encoding and image layers and does not automatically settle rights in this pinned repository serialization or any project additions.

rights.component: U-M OCR text, markup, and public item presentation
rights.basis: U-M's current CME collection presentation states that materials are in the public domain and applies the DPLA No Copyright–United States statement.
notes: The 2006 XML also carries a generic statement that copyright or distribution restrictions may apply. Both provider statements are retained pending item-level clarification.
No Copyright–United States is jurisdiction-specific, not a worldwide license.

rights.component: U-M page images of the 1909 public-domain volume
rights.basis: The current CME collection applies No Copyright–United States to its materials, while the source volume was published in 1909. The legacy XML's generic restriction warning remains unresolved for the TIFF delivery layer.
notes: Ask U-M to confirm the TIFF-specific scope before bulk redistribution.

rights.component: Morison's 1909 edited text, introduction, annotations, and arrangement
rights.basis: The volume was first published in 1909, well before the current U.S. public-domain cutoff for published works.
notes: This U.S. conclusion does not itself determine every non-U.S. jurisdiction or rights in modern scans.

rights.component: Internet Archive PDF derivative and scan-layer metadata
rights.jurisdiction: Provider terms and intended place of reuse
rights.basis: The underlying 1909 volume is public domain in the United States, but no item-specific open license for every scan-layer file or metadata component was identified in the consulted Internet Archive record.
notes: Do not transfer rights assertions from the historical edition automatically to all modern platform files.

rights.component: Fifteenth-century Middle English and Latin textual content embodied in the physical codex
rights.basis: The underlying medieval text predates modern copyright by centuries. Physical ownership, access control, modern catalog text, and manuscript photography remain separate layers.

rights.component: Trinity College manuscript photographs, IIIF manifest, and cached image bundle
rights.basis: The exact IIIF manifest attributes the object to Trinity College Cambridge and applies CC BY-NC 4.0. Trinity's Wren Digital Library guidance separately asks researchers to request permission for publication and says charges depend on the nature and use of the images.
notes: Commercial print-on-demand or repository redistribution outside the noncommercial license requires separate authorization.
The complete local bundle remains gitignored and should be treated as a private research cache unless reuse permission is clear.

rights.component: Current Trinity manuscript catalog description and website presentation
rights.basis: No open license for the prose catalog description was identified on the item page; the IIIF image license must not be assumed to license the separate catalog text.

rights.component: Wharton's 1688 preface, partial transcription, glossary, and printed arrangement
rights.basis: The work was published in 1688 and predates modern copyright.

rights.jurisdiction: Provider terms and intended place of reuse
rights.basis: The OTA record links the page images separately from the CC0 encoding, and access to the image platform requires a subscription. No open image license was identified.
notes: The CC0 dedication for A53931 text and markup does not extend to EEBO source images.

rights.component: A53931 keyboarded text and markup only; source page images excluded
rights.jurisdiction: Worldwide dedication to the extent legally possible
rights.basis: The OTA item and embedded availability statement release the Phase I keyboarded text and markup under Creative Commons CC0 1.0 and expressly permit commercial and noncommercial reuse without permission.
notes: This record governs the digital transcription layer, not the 44 source images.

rights.component: Kelly's 2021 article as a scholarly expression
rights.jurisdiction: United States and other applicable jurisdictions
rights.basis: The article was published in 2021, and no Creative Commons or other open-reuse license was identified on the eScholarship item. Public reading access does not by itself authorize redistribution.
The downloadable PDF carries the copyrighted article and no separate open redistribution license was identified. It is cached for private source validation, not repository publication.

rights.component: The official Circular 15A text and PDF delivery file
rights.basis: The circular is an official U.S. Copyright Office publication and is publicly downloadable, but this manifest did not verify an item-specific rights statement or separately document the statutory federal-government-work rule for the delivery file.

editorial_practices.description: The electronic text transcribes title matter and the complete surviving Book of Faith but leaves most preliminaries and all back matter as omitted-page placeholders.
editorial_practices.representation: Text for selected pages plus PB milestones for omitted pages

editorial_practices.description: Morison states that the punctuation is entirely his own and is intended as interpretive help for Pecock's involved syntax.
editorial_practices.scope: complete surviving edited text, printed pp.109–305
notes: The edition is not a purely diplomatic facsimile even though spelling is largely retained.

editorial_practices.description: Morison occasionally compares a reading with Wharton's partial 1688 transcript.
editorial_practices.representation: Isolated notes such as the reported Wharton reading at printed p.236
notes: No claim of systematic collation or continuous dependence on Wharton is made.

editorial_practices.description: Wharton omitted the prologue and most of Part I, retained selected Part I fragments, and printed Part II complete only as far as the imperfect witness allowed.
editorial_practices.source_feature: The surviving Book of Faith in B.14.45
editorial_practices.representation: Selected excerpts and one continuous surviving part

Its operative language repeatedly scopes the assertion to software and does not expressly identify inherited U-M XML, data, transcription, encoding, or markup or override upstream terms.

open_questions.question: Does the pinned CME repository's root Unlicense expressly cover the inherited U-M XML data, transcription, encoding, and markup at CME/source/CME_phase_1-2/ajh1649.xml?
next_steps: Seek a path-specific licensing or provenance statement from the CME repository maintainers.
Check upstream U-M terms and repository history for an explicit grant covering the imported XML serialization and any later repository additions.
Until then, retain unknown artifact-level status and do not transfer the root software license to the U-M encoding, Morison edition, page images, or other upstream layers.

open_questions.question: How does Morison's legacy 1a–129b foliation map surface by surface to Trinity's current f.1r–126v IIIF sequence?
next_steps: Collate all 251 XML milestones against the 252 current folio images while preserving the two missing leaves in quire 2 as physical lacunae.

Test whether the two missing quire-2 leaves explain two of the three folio numbers between legacy 129b and current 126v, and do not force the one further discrepancy into an assumed renumbering without inspecting every anomaly.
notes: The two systems describe one witness; they are not separate witnesses.

open_questions.question: How much text and how many original chapters were lost after current f.126v?
next_steps: Compare internal cross-references, related Pecock works, Wharton's selection, and current scholarship.
Retain Morison's estimate of at least two lost chapters as an attributed minimum rather than a recovered original total.

open_questions.question: Can U-M provide a stable complete download of the 316 TIFFs and clarify the exact rights scope for OCR/XML versus page images?
next_steps: Ask U-M for the complete image bundle and item-level production record.
Ask whether No Copyright–United States applies to both the OCR/XML and TIFF delivery layer despite the generic 2006 restriction warning.

open_questions.question: Will Trinity authorize redistribution of the complete B.14.45 image bundle in a repository supporting commercial and noncommercial print-on-demand publication?
next_steps: Email wren.library@trin.cam.ac.uk with the exact Git/POD use case and request written permission beyond CC BY-NC where necessary.
Keep the complete cache private and gitignored unless the provider license and any additional permission clearly authorize redistribution.

open_questions.question: Can the work's exact place of composition be distinguished from Pecock's residence and the mainly London audience argued in scholarship?
next_steps: Consult recent Pecock chronology and textual scholarship before assigning London or another settlement as a composition place.

review_notes: The primary transmission path is repository artifact → U-M OCR encoding → U-M page-image set → Morison 1909 edition → Trinity B.14.45. The independent Internet Archive scan and complete Trinity IIIF object are supporting facsimile routes.

Wharton 1688 is a passage-scoped historical intermediary: it directly transcribes selected Part I material and the surviving Part II from B.14.45. Morison's relation to it is limited to explicit selected apparatus comparisons; B.14.45 remains Morison's continuous source.
The 1688 print expression, its subscription-gated EEBO source images, and the public CC0 EEBO-TCP encoding are separate entities with separate rights and access statements.
The current 126-folio Trinity inventory and Morison/U-M 1a–129b milestones remain parallel. The manifest does not invent a second witness or a forced exact crosswalk.

Every cached object is complete relative to its provider source: the full Morison volume, complete Internet Archive item-metadata JSON, full A53931 XML, full Kelly article, complete four-page Copyright Office circular, and all 260 Trinity canvases. Only the Trinity cache is a complete physical-manuscript surrogate, and the manuscript itself remains textually incomplete.

Kelly's article is provisionally typed catalog_description because schema 1.0.0 has no dedicated scholarship or study entity type; its PDF is modeled as a separate delivery facsimile and does not enter the textual transmission path.

The repository artifact's root Unlicense evidence, U-M's current No Copyright–United States assertion, and the historical source layers remain separate. The software-worded root license is not treated as blanket permission for inherited U-M XML or later repository serialization additions.
